title: Signs You Need a New Roof: What Every Homeowner Should Know
excerpt: Missing shingles, granules in gutters, and daylight through the attic are all warning signs. Here's how to know when it's time for a roof replacement.
content:
Your roof protects everything underneath it, but most homeowners don't think about it until there's a problem. Here are the top signs it might be time for a replacement:

**1. Age** — Most asphalt shingle roofs last 20-25 years. If yours is approaching that age, it's time for an inspection.

**2. Missing or curling shingles** — Wind and UV exposure degrade shingles over time. Missing shingles leave your decking exposed to moisture.

**3. Granules in gutters** — Those dark granules protect shingles from UV. Heavy granule loss means your roof is nearing the end of its life.

**4. Daylight through the attic** — If you can see light through your roof boards, water can get through too.

**5. Sagging areas** — This indicates structural damage to the decking and requires immediate attention.

If you notice any of these signs, contact us for a free roof inspection. We'll give you an honest assessment and help you understand your options — including insurance coverage if storm damage is involved.

title: Siding Comparison: Vinyl vs. Fiber Cement vs. Engineered Wood
excerpt: Choosing new siding? We break down the pros, cons, cost, and longevity of the three most popular siding materials for {{CITY}} homes.
content:
When it's time for new siding, {{CITY}} homeowners typically choose between three options. Here's how they compare:

## Vinyl Siding
**Cost:** $$ | **Lifespan:** 20-30 years
- Most affordable option
- Low maintenance (no painting)
- Wide range of colors and styles
- Can warp in extreme heat

## Fiber Cement (James Hardie)
**Cost:** $$$ | **Lifespan:** 30-50 years
- Exceptional durability
- Resists fire, rot, and insects
- Holds paint well
- Heavier, requires professional installation

## Engineered Wood (LP SmartSide)
**Cost:** $$$ | **Lifespan:** 25-40 years
- Natural wood appearance
- Lighter than fiber cement
- Treated to resist moisture and insects
- Requires periodic painting

## Our Recommendation
For most {{CITY}} homeowners, **fiber cement** offers the best long-term value. However, **vinyl** is an excellent choice for budget-conscious projects, and **engineered wood** is perfect if you want that authentic wood look without the maintenance of real wood.

Contact us for a free estimate on any siding material — we'll help you choose what's right for your home and budget.

title: Why Attic Insulation Is the Best Investment for {{CITY}} Homeowners
excerpt: Most homes in {{STATE}} are under-insulated. Upgrading your attic insulation can cut heating costs by 15-25% and pays for itself within a few winters.
content:
If your heating bills keep climbing, the problem might be above your head — literally. Most homes in {{CITY}} were built with insulation that doesn't meet today's energy codes.

## The Numbers
- The DOE recommends **R-49 to R-60** for attics in our climate zone
- Most older homes have **R-19 or less**
- Proper insulation can reduce heating costs by **15-25%**

## Signs Your Attic Needs More Insulation
1. **Uneven temperatures** between rooms
2. **Ice dams** forming on your roof in winter
3. **High energy bills** compared to similar homes
4. **Insulation below the floor joists** — you should not be able to see the joists

## Types of Attic Insulation
- **Blown-in fiberglass** — Most common, excellent coverage
- **Blown-in cellulose** — Good for existing homes, fills gaps well
- **Spray foam** — Best air sealing, highest R-value per inch

## The Bottom Line
Attic insulation is one of the fastest-payback home improvements you can make. Most projects pay for themselves in 2-4 heating seasons through energy savings alone.

We offer free attic inspections and can tell you exactly where you stand. Contact us to schedule yours.
